title: Kanboard vs Trello for Solo users
meta_description: Kanboard vs Trello for solo users who want a stable kanban board without self-hosting or plugin upkeep.
constraint_lens: You want a kanban board that works reliably without self-hosting, plugin updates, or technical upkeep.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for solo users who refuse ongoing maintenance.
one_second_verdict.reason: Kanboard fails first because self-hosting and plugin maintenance require periodic technical upkeep.

verdict.summary: Trello wins for solo users who refuse ongoing maintenance. It runs as a hosted service where boards work without managing servers or plugins. Kanboard often involves self-hosting and updating plugins for extended features. If self-hosting and plugin maintenance require periodic technical upkeep, Kanboard fails first.
verdict.decision_rule: If self-hosting and plugin maintenance require periodic technical upkeep, Kanboard fails first.

sections.heading: Best fit for zero-maintenance boards
sections.content: You host personal projects and refuse to maintain plugin updates or server setups. Trello operates as a hosted web app with automatic updates handled by the provider. Kanboard is commonly self-hosted, which can involve server configuration and plugin management.

bullets.why_it_matters: You control your own server and data environment, but must manage updates yourself.
You add functionality such as reporting or automation, though this may require version compatibility checks.

bullets.point: Minimal core interface without forced integrations
bullets.why_it_matters: You run a lean kanban board, but maintenance remains your responsibility.

bullets.point: Fully hosted service with automatic updates
bullets.why_it_matters: Boards stay operational without managing servers or software versions.
You create lists and move cards without configuring hosting environments.

bullets.point: Optional power-ups managed through the platform
bullets.why_it_matters: You enable extra features without manually updating plugins on a server.

sections.heading: Where each tool can break down

items.fails_when: You must apply security patches, update plugins, or troubleshoot hosting issues.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Trello for a board that requires no server management.

items.fails_when: You require full control over hosting and custom plugin development.
items.what_to_do_instead: Switch to Kanboard if self-hosting control outweighs maintenance concerns.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: If you already maintain a server for other projects and are comfortable handling updates, Kanboard may feel manageable.

sections.heading: Quick rules
rules: If you want zero technical upkeep, choose Trello.
If server and plugin updates sound like ongoing work, avoid Kanboard.
If full hosting control matters more than maintenance load, consider Kanboard.

faqs.a: It is often self-hosted, which means you manage server updates and maintenance.
Yes. Trello runs as a web service with updates handled automatically.

faqs.q: Which is easier for a solo user with no technical upkeep?
faqs.a: Trello is easier because it does not require server or plugin maintenance.
It makes sense if you need full hosting control and are comfortable maintaining it.
